answers.value:
The "Riverbend Vision & Wellness Mobile Clinic Initiative" is unique in our district because it combines two global causes—vision and diabetes—into a single, recurring mobile outreach model that is fully integrated with our local health system and school district.

INTEGRATED GLOBAL CAUSES
Instead of running separate vision screenings and diabetes awareness events, our club designed a one-stop mobile clinic that offers:
- Free visual acuity screenings for all ages
- On-site non-mydriatic retinal photography for adults at risk of diabetic retinopathy
- Random blood glucose testing and A1C vouchers for high‑risk individuals
- Blood pressure checks and BMI measurements
- Immediate referrals to partner optometrists and a community health center

This integrated approach directly supports Lions’ global causes of vision and diabetes by:
- Identifying undiagnosed vision impairment and diabetic retinopathy early
- Connecting high‑risk individuals with preventive care and education
- Reducing barriers to care in low-income neighborhoods where transportation and insurance are major obstacles

INNOVATIVE MOBILE MODEL
What sets the project apart is the way we configured a used 26-foot step van into a fully functional mobile clinic using mostly donated equipment and volunteer labor. The van was donated by a local delivery company and retrofitted on weekends by club members who have trades skills (electricians, carpenters, and an HVAC technician). We installed:
- Two compact screening stations with foldable privacy partitions
- A small refrigeration unit for certain medical supplies
- A portable handwashing sink with a grey-water tank
- A quiet generator and solar panels on the roof to reduce fuel use and noise

We developed a detailed setup protocol so that within 30 minutes of arrival, the team can be ready to see the first participant. We also created a digital intake workflow using tablets and a simple cloud-based system that allows us to log screenings, risk factors, and referrals on-site without using paper forms.

DATA-DRIVEN PLANNING
Before launching, we analyzed local public health data and school nurse reports to identify neighborhoods with the highest rates of uninsured residents, missed vision screenings, and diabetes-related ER visits. Using this information, we prioritized three zip codes that have a median household income 30–40% below the city average.

To exceed global cause goals, we set very clear targets for the first 12 months:
- Screen at least 1,000 individuals for vision issues
- Provide diabetes risk assessments for at least 700 adults
- Achieve a 50% follow‑through rate on referrals for comprehensive eye exams
- Host at least 10 combined Vision & Diabetes mobile clinic days

We surpassed all of these goals in the first year:
- 1,384 individuals received vision screenings (451 children, 933 adults)
- 912 adults received diabetes risk assessments
- 62% of those referred for comprehensive eye exams attended their appointments within 60 days (verified through our partner clinics)

HOLISTIC SUPPORT AND FOLLOW‑UP
Another innovative aspect is our emphasis on follow‑through instead of one‑time screenings. For example:
- When we identify a child who fails a vision screening, our club covers the cost of a full eye exam and a basic pair of glasses through a voucher system we set up with two local optometrists.
- For adults who screen at high risk for diabetes or pre-diabetes, we provide instant scheduling support with the Riverbend Community Health Center, including help filling out sliding-scale fee applications.
- We partnered with the school district’s family liaison team to follow up with families who missed appointments, offering transportation assistance via donated bus passes.

COMMUNITY-SPECIFIC ADAPTATIONS
The project is also unique because it is linguistically and culturally tailored. Riverbend has a large Spanish-speaking and growing Somali community. Our club recruited bilingual and bicultural volunteers, including two Lions who are certified medical interpreters. All educational materials are available in English, Spanish, and Somali, and we host separate small‑group sessions in each language explaining:
- Why early detection of vision problems is critical for school performance
- How diabetes can silently damage vision and other organs
- Simple, culturally appropriate dietary changes (e.g., modifying traditional dishes rather than replacing them)

We intentionally scheduled events in locations that already feel safe and familiar to families: school parking lots on parent-teacher conference days, church and mosque parking lots after services, and outside the public library on Saturdays.

INNOVATION DURING COVID AND BEYOND
This initiative grew out of our pandemic response. During COVID-19, many families missed routine school screenings and checkups. We piloted a small-scale mobile vision screening in 2023 and learned that families trusted us when we came directly to their neighborhoods. Building on that, the current project expanded the concept into a permanent mobile clinic that continues to operate even as the pandemic has receded, addressing the backlog of unmet care.

In summary, this project is outstanding because it:
- Integrates multiple global causes in one coordinated model
- Uses data-driven planning to target the highest-need communities
- Emphasizes follow-through and care coordination, not just one-time events
- Is culturally and linguistically tailored to our local population
- Repurposes donated equipment creatively to maximize impact with limited funds.

answers.value:
Riverbend is a small post-industrial city with about 65,000 residents. Over the past decade, our two remaining manufacturing plants downsized, and many families lost employer-sponsored health insurance. According to our county health department, roughly 18% of adults in our city are uninsured, and 11% have been diagnosed with diabetes, with an even higher estimated rate of undiagnosed cases.

VISION AND EDUCATION IMPACT
Before our project, school nurses reported that a significant number of students were struggling to see the board but were not following up with eye exams due to cost and transportation. One middle school counselor shared that several students were being evaluated for learning disabilities when, in reality, they simply could not see clearly.

Through the mobile clinic, we identified 126 children who failed their initial vision screening. Of those, 104 received full eye exams and glasses funded through our program. Teachers later reported noticeable improvements:
- A 6th-grade teacher at Riverbend Middle School told us that one student, Miguel, who had been quiet and disengaged, began participating in class once he received glasses. His reading scores increased by nearly two grade levels over the school year.
- Another student, Amina, had been frequently sent to the office for headaches. After she received glasses, her headache complaints dropped dramatically, and she started attending school more consistently.

These individual stories illustrate the broader community importance: by addressing basic vision needs, we’re directly supporting educational success, reducing misdiagnosis of learning problems, and improving quality of life for children.

DIABETES AND CHRONIC DISEASE BURDEN
On the adult side, uncontrolled diabetes has been a major driver of emergency room visits and preventable complications in Riverbend. Our county hospital shared de-identified data showing that our zip codes have one of the highest rates of diabetes-related ER visits in the state.

At our mobile clinics:
- 912 adults participated in diabetes risk assessments.
- 217 individuals had blood glucose results or risk profiles that indicated a strong likelihood of pre-diabetes or undiagnosed diabetes.
- 189 of these high-risk individuals accepted immediate referrals to the Riverbend Community Health Center.

One participant, a 52-year-old former factory worker named James, came to our event at the public library simply because he saw the Lions logo and free screening sign. His blood glucose reading was over 300 mg/dL, and he had been ignoring symptoms like constant thirst and fatigue. We were able to:
- Arrange a same-week appointment at the community health center.
- Help him complete the sliding-scale fee application.
- Connect him with a free diabetes education class run by a retired nurse who is also a Lion.

Six months later, James returned to one of our events just to thank the team. His A1C had dropped significantly, he had changed his diet, and he said, "If you hadn’t been out there that Saturday, I honestly don’t know where I’d be right now."

REDUCING BARRIERS TO CARE
The importance of the project lies not just in the screenings themselves, but in how it reduces structural barriers:
- COST: Many residents cannot afford eye exams and glasses, even with discount programs. By covering the cost for children and providing deeply discounted options for adults, we remove a major obstacle.
- TRANSPORTATION: Our city’s bus system has limited weekend service, and some neighborhoods are more than 2 miles from the nearest clinic. By bringing the clinic directly into school and church parking lots, we eliminate the need for multiple bus transfers or long walks.
- TRUST: Some immigrant families are hesitant to engage with formal healthcare institutions due to language barriers or past negative experiences. Our repeated presence in familiar, community-owned spaces and our use of interpreters has built trust. Families now recognize our mobile clinic and often bring other family members when they see us return.

COMMUNITY RESILIENCE AND AWARENESS
The project has also raised general awareness about the link between chronic disease and vision. At each event, we host short, informal talks—sometimes in the back of the van, sometimes under a pop-up tent—about:
- How diabetes can lead to blindness if unmanaged
- The importance of annual eye exams, even if you feel fine
- Simple, affordable ways to reduce sugar intake without giving up culturally important foods

We’ve had people come back months later to tell us they:
- Switched from sugary sodas to water or unsweetened tea
- Started walking with neighbors in the evenings after dinner
- Encouraged family members to get screened as well

LOCAL HEALTH SYSTEM IMPACT
Because of our data-sharing agreement (with appropriate privacy protections) with the Riverbend Community Health Center and two optometry practices, the project has helped them identify unmet needs and adjust their services. For instance, one optometrist extended Saturday hours once a month specifically to accommodate our referral patients, and the health center added a bilingual diabetes educator to their staff.

In short, the project’s importance in our community can be summarized as:
- Improving educational outcomes through better vision
- Catching diabetes and related complications earlier
- Building trust in preventive healthcare among underserved populations
- Strengthening collaboration between civic organizations, schools, and healthcare providers

For a relatively small club in a mid-sized city, the mobile clinic has become a visible symbol of hope and practical help. When residents see the Lions logo on the side of the van, they now associate it with real, tangible support rather than just a one-time event.

answers.value:
We intentionally built this project as a collaborative effort rather than a standalone Lions initiative. Our key local partners include:

1. Riverbend Community Health Center (RCHC)
- Role: Primary medical partner for diabetes follow‑up care.
- Contributions:
  - Provides a nurse practitioner or registered nurse for each mobile clinic day to oversee blood glucose testing, interpret results, and make clinical referrals.
  - Reserves a small block of appointments (typically 10–15 slots per clinic day) specifically for individuals referred from the mobile clinic.
  - Offers sliding-scale payment options and assistance with Medicaid/insurance enrollment for eligible participants.
- Example: For our April 2025 clinic at St. Mary’s Parish, RCHC sent two staff members who stayed an extra hour past the event’s scheduled end time to see everyone in line.

2. Riverbend Unified School District
- Role: Host sites and outreach partner.
- Contributions:
  - Allows us to park the mobile clinic in school parking lots during parent-teacher conference evenings and back-to-school nights.
  - Helps distribute flyers and robocall messages in English, Spanish, and Somali informing families about upcoming clinics.
  - School nurses assist with vision screenings for children, particularly at the elementary level.
- Example: At Lincoln Elementary, the principal personally walked families out to the van to encourage them to participate, which significantly boosted turnout.

3. ClearView Optometry Group and Riverbend Family Eye Care (Two Local Optometry Practices)
- Role: Comprehensive eye care partners.
- Contributions:
  - Provide deeply discounted eye exams for referred patients.
  - Donate a limited number of free basic eyeglasses for children each quarter (we supplement costs through club funds for additional pairs).
  - Offer on-call consultation when we encounter unusual screening results.
- Example: ClearView Optometry donated 30 pairs of children’s frames at wholesale cost for our annual "Back-to-School Sight Day."

4. Riverbend Interfaith Council (Including St. Mary’s Catholic Church, Masjid Al-Salaam, and Grace Community Church)
- Role: Community trust bridge and event hosts.
- Contributions:
  - Provide parking lot space, access to restrooms, and indoor space for group education sessions.
  - Announce our clinics during services and in newsletters, emphasizing that services are available regardless of immigration status or insurance.
  - Recruit volunteers from their congregations to assist with registration and translation.
- Example: Masjid Al-Salaam hosted a Friday afternoon clinic during Ramadan, and we tailored our educational messaging to align with fasting practices and meal timing.

5. Riverbend Public Library
- Role: Central civic host and promotion partner.
- Contributions:
  - Hosts the mobile clinic in its main parking lot on the first Saturday of every other month.
  - Promotes events on the library website, social media, and bulletin boards.
  - Allows us to set up a small health information table inside the lobby with brochures and a sign-up sheet for follow-up classes.

6. Riverbend Transit Authority
- Role: Transportation support.
- Contributions:
  - Donates 20–30 bus passes per clinic day for participants who need transportation to follow-up appointments.
  - Works with us to align event times with bus schedules serving high-need neighborhoods.

7. Healthy Riverbend Coalition (Local Nonprofit Network)
- Role: Strategic and funding partner.
- Contributions:
  - Helped us write a small community health grant that funded the refurbishment of the mobile clinic van.
  - Provides technical assistance on data collection, evaluation, and reporting.

8. Local Businesses and Service Clubs
- Riverbend Rotary Club: Donated $2,500 toward the purchase of the retinal camera.
- Sunrise Fitness Center: Provides free 1-month trial memberships to adults who enroll in a diabetes prevention program through our clinics.
- La Estrella Market and Halal Fresh Grocers: Allow us to set up small informational displays about upcoming clinic days and distribute recipe cards for healthier versions of familiar dishes.

Each of these partners plays a specific role that we document in a simple memorandum of understanding, ensuring clear expectations and continuity even when staff or volunteers change. This network of partnerships is a key reason the project has been sustainable and impactful.

answers.value:
We made extensive use of Lions Clubs International (LCI) resources both during the planning phase and throughout implementation. Specifically:

1. MyLion® Service Reporting and Planning
- We used the MyLion platform to log each mobile clinic event, track volunteer hours, and record the number of people served (by age group and type of service—vision screening, diabetes assessment, etc.).
- This data helped us demonstrate impact to local funders and partners. For instance, when applying for a community health mini‑grant, we were able to show that in the first six months we had logged over 450 volunteer hours and served more than 600 individuals.

2. LCI Vision and Diabetes Project Planning Guides
- Our project team reviewed the LCI project planning guides for vision screening and diabetes awareness to develop our workflow and safety protocols.
- We adapted LCI’s sample checklists for:
  - Pre‑event planning (site confirmation, equipment checks, volunteer assignments)
  - On-site flow (registration, screening stations, education corner, referral desk)
  - Post‑event follow-up (data entry, partner debrief, follow-up call schedule)
- We also used LCI’s guidance on liability and risk management to ensure that our consent forms and screening disclaimers were appropriate.

3. Lions Learning Center (Online Courses)
- Several club members completed online courses related to:
  - Organizing community service projects
  - Leading effective teams
  - Serving with diversity and inclusion in mind
- Our project chair, Maria Rodriguez, used the modules on "Member Motivation" and "Team Collaboration" to structure volunteer roles so that newer members felt supported and not overwhelmed.

4. LCI Health Education Materials
- We downloaded and printed LCI brochures and posters on:
  - Diabetic eye disease
  - The importance of regular eye exams
  - Basic diabetes prevention tips
- We translated key points from these materials into Spanish and Somali (with the help of bilingual Lions and community partners) while retaining the core LCI messaging and branding.
- The recognizable Lions logo on these materials helped build credibility, especially for residents who were unfamiliar with our local club but had seen Lions’ work online or in other cities.

5. District-Level Support and Grants
- With guidance from our district Global Service Team (GST) coordinator, we applied for and received a small district-level grant that covered part of the cost of refurbishing the mobile clinic van.
- The district leadership also connected us with another club in a neighboring city that had run a smaller-scale mobile screening project. We held a joint Zoom call where they shared lessons learned about equipment maintenance, data tracking, and volunteer scheduling.

6. LCI Marketing Resources
- We used LCI’s customizable publicity templates to create flyers, social media posts, and press releases for our mobile clinic launch.
- We adapted a Lions-branded banner design to create a large, durable sign for the side of the van that reads: "Lions Mobile Vision & Wellness Clinic – Serving Riverbend."

Overall, LCI’s resources allowed us to avoid "reinventing the wheel." Instead, we started from strong, evidence-based frameworks and then tailored them to our community’s specific needs and languages. This not only made planning more efficient but also ensured that our project aligned closely with Lions’ global service priorities and reporting standards.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
SUSTAINABILITY AND FUTURE PLANS
From the outset, we designed the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Mobile Clinic Initiative to be a multi-year effort rather than a one-off project. To ensure sustainability, we:
- Created a dedicated project committee within the club, including a succession plan so that newer members can gradually assume leadership roles.
- Established a small restricted fund within our club’s service budget specifically for mobile clinic operations (fuel, basic maintenance, replacement supplies).
- Are exploring the possibility of applying for a Lions Clubs International Foundation (LCIF) grant in the next cycle to expand services to neighboring towns that share similar health challenges.

We also plan to:
- Add hearing screenings at selected events by partnering with a local audiologist who has expressed interest.
- Increase our focus on youth by coordinating with the school district to ensure that every 3rd- and 6th-grade student in the three highest-need schools receives a vision screening either at school or through the mobile clinic.

CLUB CAPACITY BUILDING
This project has transformed our club’s culture and visibility. Before the mobile clinic, our service was more event-based (pancake breakfasts, holiday food baskets), which were valuable but less directly tied to Lions’ global causes. The mobile clinic has:
- Attracted seven new members in the past year, including two healthcare professionals and three younger members in their 20s and early 30s.
- Encouraged long-time members to update their skills, such as learning to use digital tablets for intake or taking blood pressure readings.
- Given our club a clear, signature project that we are known for throughout the city.

PERSONAL NOTE FROM THE CLUB SERVICE CHAIRPERSON
As the club service chairperson, I (Maria Rodriguez) grew up in Riverbend and remember my own parents struggling to afford glasses for me when I was in elementary school. I still remember the first time I put on my new glasses and realized that trees had individual leaves. That memory has been a driving force behind my commitment to this project.

During one of our early clinics, a little girl named Sofia, about 8 years old, came into the van with her mother. She had been squinting at the board in class and complaining of headaches. When we did her screening, it was clear she needed a full exam and likely glasses. Her mother shared, in Spanish, that they had been putting off the appointment because of cost and because her husband’s work hours made scheduling difficult.

We were able to:
- Schedule an exam for Sofia at ClearView Optometry during an evening slot.
- Provide a voucher that covered the cost of the exam and glasses.
- Arrange for her mother to receive a bus pass for transportation.

Two months later, Sofia ran up to our table at a school event wearing bright purple glasses and proudly told us, "I can see everything now!" Her mother had tears in her eyes and simply said, "Gracias."

That moment, and many others like it, remind us why this work matters so deeply. It is not just about numbers in a report; it is about helping our neighbors live, learn, and work with dignity and health.

We are honored to nominate the Riverbend Community Lions Club’s Vision & Wellness Mobile Clinic Initiative for the Kindness Matters Service Award and hope that our experience can inspire and inform other clubs that wish to develop similar integrated, community-based projects aligned with Lions’ global causes.
